title: Dog Walking Unsteadily After Mushroom Ingestion: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is walking unsteadily after eating a mushroom, this is a high-urgency emergency. Learn the risks and immediate steps to take.
content_html:
TL;DR: Walking unsteadily after eating a mushroom is a life-threatening veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention. Take a clear photo of the fungi to help your veterinarian identify the specific toxins and determine the best course of treatment.

What does it mean if my dog is walking unsteadily after eating a mushroom?

When a dog walks unsteadily—often described as appearing 'drunk' or wobbly—after consuming a mushroom, it is a clinical sign known as ataxia. This suggests that the mushroom contained toxins that are affecting the dog's central nervous system, liver, or kidneys. Mushroom poisoning in canines can range from mild gastrointestinal upset to fatal organ failure depending on the species of fungi ingested.

Is it a veterinary emergency if my dog is wobbly after eating a mushroom?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a critical veterinary emergency.
  • Neurological symptoms like unsteadiness, tremors, or disorientation indicate that toxins have already entered the bloodstream and are affecting bodily functions.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately.
  • Do not wait for the symptoms to clear on their own, as some mushroom toxins cause irreversible organ damage within hours.

How does taking a photo of the mushroom help with my dog's treatment?

  • Identification is the most important factor in determining the course of treatment.
  • If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of the mushroom your dog consumed, ensuring you capture the cap, the gills underneath, and the base of the stem.
  • This photo allows veterinarians or mycologists to identify the specific toxin (such as amanitin or muscarine).
  • Accurate identification dictates whether your dog needs specific antidotes, aggressive IV fluids, or supportive neurological care.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following mushroom ingestion in dogs, clinical findings of concern include ataxia, vomiting, abdominal pain, salivation, lacrimation, watery diarrhea, miosis, bronchoconstriction, bradycardia, arrhythmias, hypotension or hypertension, and shock, often associated with cholinergic stimulation. Isoxazole-induced toxicity can cause coma, while psilocin-induced toxicity may not. Diagnosis is based on a history of mushroom ingestion, identification of the suspected mushroom, and consistent clinical signs. Treatment involves supportive care, including fluid and electrolyte replacement. In severe, life-threatening cases, atropine therapy (0.2-2 mg/kg, a portion IV and the remaining IM or SC, repeated as necessary) may be administered. Diazepam (0.5-1 mg/kg IV, with incremental dose increases of 5-10 mg to effect) or phenobarbital (6 mg/kg to effect) can be given for symptomatic control, and body temperature should be monitored.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3098)

faqs.question: How quickly do mushroom toxins affect dogs?
faqs.answer: Symptoms can appear as quickly as 15 to 30 minutes for some species, while others, like the deadly Death Cap, may cause a delay of 6 to 12 hours before severe symptoms manifest.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: Never induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a licensed veterinarian. If your dog is already walking unsteadily, they may be at risk of inhaling vomit into their lungs (aspiration).

faqs.question: What if I can't find the mushroom they ate?
faqs.answer: If you cannot find a sample or photo, go to the vet immediately anyway. The clinical signs (unsteadiness, drooling, or vomiting) will help the vet provide the necessary supportive care even without a species identification.
